title: Quiz: Thermal Management
description: Test your understanding of thermal contact, Kapitza resistance, and heat sinking techniques in cryogenic systems

questions.question: What is Kapitza resistance and why is it important at millikelvin temperatures?

options.text: Electrical resistance that increases at low temperature, causing Joule heating
Thermal boundary resistance due to acoustic mismatch, scales as T^-3 and dominates below 1 K
Resistance to magnetic field penetration in superconductors
Mechanical resistance that prevents thermal contact between surfaces

questions.explanation: Kapitza resistance is a thermal boundary resistance arising from the acoustic mismatch between materials at interfaces. It scales as T^-3, meaning it increases by 1000x between 100 mK and 10 mK, making it the dominant thermal resistance at millikelvin temperatures.

questions.question: Which technique is most effective for improving thermal contact at cryogenic temperatures?

options.text: Using thermal grease (Apiezon N) at all temperatures
Pressing surfaces together harder without any interface material
Using indium foil between gold-plated surfaces with appropriate pressure
Relying on the natural contact between machined surfaces

questions.explanation: Indium foil is soft and deforms to fill surface irregularities, greatly increasing actual contact area. Gold plating prevents oxide formation. Together, this combination provides 10-100x improvement in thermal contact compared to bare surfaces.

questions.question: How can Kapitza resistance be reduced at millikelvin temperatures?

options.text: By using smoother surfaces
By greatly increasing the surface area (e.g., sintered metal powder)
By operating at higher temperatures
By using thicker interface materials

questions.explanation: Since Kapitza resistance scales as R_K = A_K / (T^3 * Area), increasing the surface area is the most effective way to reduce it. Sintered metal powders can increase effective surface area by 1000x or more.

questions.question: In the thermal-electrical analogy, what is the thermal equivalent of electrical current?

options.text: Temperature
Thermal resistance
Heat flow (power)
Heat capacity

questions.explanation: In the thermal-electrical analogy: Temperature corresponds to voltage, heat flow (power) corresponds to current, thermal resistance corresponds to electrical resistance, and heat capacity corresponds to capacitance.

questions.question: At very low temperatures, electron-phonon thermalization times can become long enough (milliseconds to seconds) that they must be considered in measurement protocols.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True! The electron-phonon thermalization time tau scales strongly with temperature. At millikelvin temperatures, this can become milliseconds to even seconds, meaning the system requires significant time to reach thermal equilibrium after any disturbance.

questions.question: Why is OFHC (Oxygen-Free High Conductivity) copper preferred for cryogenic thermal management?

options.text: It is the cheapest material available
It has very high purity, leading to high thermal conductivity at low temperatures
It becomes superconducting at cryogenic temperatures
It has lower thermal conductivity than regular copper

questions.explanation: OFHC copper has very high purity (indicated by high RRR values of 100-500), which means fewer impurity scattering centers for electrons. This results in very high thermal conductivity at low temperatures, often higher than at room temperature.

questions.question: Which of the following materials are appropriate for THERMAL ISOLATION in cryogenic systems? (Select all that apply)

options.text: G10 fiberglass composite
OFHC copper
Vespel (polyimide)
Stainless steel (thin-walled tubes)

questions.explanation: G10, Vespel, and stainless steel all have low thermal conductivity and are used for thermal isolation. OFHC copper has very HIGH thermal conductivity and is used for heat spreading and thermal contact, not isolation.
